title: AWS Lambda function with TypeScript and API Gateway integration
AWS CDK stack for Lambda with DynamoDB and API Gateway
CloudFormation vs CDK vs Terraform: infrastructure-as-code comparison
body:
Three dominant IaC tools for AWS with different abstractions and tradeoffs.

**CloudFormation** — native AWS, declarative YAML/JSON:
```yaml
Resources:
  UsersTable:
    Type: AWS::DynamoDB::Table
    Properties:
      TableName: users
      BillingMode: PAY_PER_REQUEST
      AttributeDefinitions:
        - AttributeName: pk
          AttributeType: S
      KeySchema:
        - AttributeName: pk
          KeyType: HASH
```

**CDK** — TypeScript/Python over CloudFormation:
```typescript
const table = new dynamodb.Table(this, 'Users', {
  partitionKey: { name: 'pk', type: dynamodb.AttributeType.STRING },
  billingMode: dynamodb.BillingMode.PAY_PER_REQUEST,
});
```

**Terraform** — multi-cloud, HCL syntax:
```hcl
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "users" {
  name         = "users"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "pk"

  attribute {
    name = "pk"
    type = "S"
  }
}
```

**Key differences:** CloudFormation has the deepest AWS integration but verbose syntax and slow rollbacks. CDK generates CloudFormation but adds type safety, constructs, and programming language power. Terraform supports multi-cloud (AWS, GCP, Azure) with a mature state management system and larger provider ecosystem. CDK has the fastest development velocity for AWS-only projects. Terraform is the standard choice for multi-cloud or when team members already know HCL. CloudFormation is best when you need zero external dependencies.

AWS CDK bootstrap error and cross-account deployment issues
body:
CDK requires bootstrapping each account/region combination before deploying. Missing bootstrap causes cryptic deployment errors.

**Error:**
```
❌ Deployment failed: Error: This stack uses assets, so the toolkit stack must be deployed
to the environment (aws://123456789/us-east-1)
```

**Fix — bootstrap the environment:**
```bash
# Bootstrap default account/region
cdk bootstrap

# Bootstrap specific account/region
cdk bootstrap aws://123456789/us-east-1

# Bootstrap with custom qualifier (for multiple CDK apps)
cdk bootstrap --qualifier myapp

# Bootstrap for cross-account deployment (CI/CD)
cdk bootstrap aws://222222222222/us-east-1 \
  --trust 111111111111 \
  --trust-for-lookup 111111111111 \
  --cloudformation-execution-policies arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess
```

**Cross-account deployment setup:**
```typescript
// bin/app.ts
const devEnv = { account: '111111111111', region: 'us-east-1' };
const prodEnv = { account: '222222222222', region: 'us-east-1' };

new MyStack(app, 'DevStack', { env: devEnv });
new MyStack(app, 'ProdStack', { env: prodEnv });
```

**Common bootstrap errors:**
1. `CDKToolkit stack already exists` — run `cdk bootstrap` again to update it
2. `Access Denied` — ensure your IAM user/role has CloudFormation and S3 permissions
3. `Qualifier mismatch` — use `--qualifier` flag consistently

The bootstrap stack creates an S3 bucket for assets, an ECR repository for Docker images, and IAM roles for deployment. CDK v2 uses modern bootstrap by default. Re-bootstrap periodically to get security updates.

title: AWS SDK v3 for JavaScript: modular imports and middleware
Terraform state drift detection and remediation strategies
body:
State drift occurs when infrastructure changes outside Terraform (manual console changes, other tools, or auto-scaling). Drift makes `terraform plan` unreliable.

**Detect drift:**
```bash
# Compare state with actual infrastructure
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Detailed refresh
terraform refresh  # DEPRECATED: use plan -refresh-only instead

# Show current state
terraform show
terraform state list
terraform state show aws_lambda_function.api
```

**Common drift scenarios and fixes:**
```bash
# Scenario 1: Resource was manually deleted
# Remove from state (doesn't delete, already gone)
terraform state rm aws_s3_bucket.old_bucket

# Scenario 2: Resource was manually modified
# Option A: Let Terraform fix it
terraform apply  # Will revert manual changes

# Option B: Accept manual changes into state
terraform apply -refresh-only  # Updates state to match reality

# Scenario 3: Import manually-created resource
terraform import aws_dynamodb_table.users users-table

# Scenario 4: Move resource between modules
terraform state mv 'module.old.aws_lambda_function.api' 'module.new.aws_lambda_function.api'
```

**Prevention:**
```hcl
# Prevent modifications to critical resources
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "users" {
  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
    ignore_changes  = [tags["LastModifiedBy"]]  # Ignore auto-set tags
  }
}
```

Run `terraform plan -refresh-only` in CI weekly to detect drift. Use AWS Config rules to alert on changes not matching Terraform tags. Always tag Terraform-managed resources with `ManagedBy = "terraform"` to discourage manual changes.

How to implement CI/CD for AWS CDK with GitHub Actions
body:
Deploy CDK stacks from GitHub Actions using OIDC authentication (no long-lived access keys) and environment-based approvals.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

jobs:
  diff:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci

      - name: Configure AWS credentials
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/github-actions-deploy
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: CDK Diff
        run: npx cdk diff -c stage=production 2>&1 | tee cdk-diff.txt

      - name: Comment PR with diff
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const diff = fs.readFileSync('cdk-diff.txt', 'utf8');
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `## CDK Diff\n\`\`\`\n${diff.substring(0, 60000)}\n\`\`\``,
            });

  deploy:
    needs: diff
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production  # Requires approval
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: '20', cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/github-actions-deploy
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - run: npx cdk deploy --all -c stage=production --require-approval never
```

OIDC eliminates AWS access keys in GitHub secrets. The `environment: production` gate requires manual approval in GitHub. `--require-approval never` is safe in CI because the diff step already shows changes on the PR.

title: Terraform AWS provider with assume role for cross-account access
body:
Cross-account Terraform access uses IAM role assumption. This pattern is essential for organizations with separate accounts for dev, staging, and production.

```hcl
# providers.tf
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"

  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::${var.target_account_id}:role/TerraformDeployRole"
    session_name = "terraform-${var.environment}"
    external_id  = var.external_id  # Optional, for third-party access
  }

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    }
  }
}

# Multi-region support
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "us_west_2"
  region = "us-west-2"

  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::${var.target_account_id}:role/TerraformDeployRole"
  }
}
```

**IAM role in target account (CloudFormation):**
```yaml
Resources:
  TerraformDeployRole:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      RoleName: TerraformDeployRole
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              AWS: arn:aws:iam::111111111111:root  # CI/CD account
            Action: sts:AssumeRole
            Condition:
              StringEquals:
                sts:ExternalId: !Ref ExternalId
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess  # Scope down in production
```

**Use resources in different regions:**
```hcl
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "west_backup" {
  provider = aws.us_west_2
  bucket   = "myapp-backup-us-west-2"
}
```

The `assume_role` block handles STS `AssumeRole` calls automatically. Provider aliases enable multi-region deployments in a single Terraform configuration.

title: DynamoDB conditional writes for optimistic locking
AWS Lambda running in VPC adds significant cold start latency
AWS Well-Architected cost optimization checklist for serverless
body:
A practical checklist for reducing AWS serverless costs based on Well-Architected Framework recommendations.

**Lambda optimization:**
```bash
# 1. Right-size memory (use Power Tuning)
# 2. Use ARM64 architecture (20% cheaper)
# 3. Minimize package size (tree-shake, external AWS SDK)
# 4. Set appropriate timeouts (don't leave at 15min default)
```

```typescript
// Optimized Lambda config
new nodejs.NodejsFunction(this, 'Fn', {
  architecture: lambda.Architecture.ARM_64,
  memorySize: 512,  // Determined via Power Tuning
  timeout: cdk.Duration.seconds(10),  // Not 900
  bundling: {
    minify: true,
    externalModules: ['@aws-sdk/*'],
  },
});
```

**DynamoDB optimization:**
```typescript
// Use on-demand for unpredictable, provisioned+autoscaling for steady
// Enable TTL for expiring data (free deletion)
const table = new dynamodb.Table(this, 'Table', {
  billingMode: dynamodb.BillingMode.PAY_PER_REQUEST,
  timeToLiveAttribute: 'ttl',  // Auto-delete expired items for free
});
```

**API Gateway optimization:**
- Use HTTP API ($1/million) instead of REST API ($3.50/million) when possible
- Enable caching on REST API for read-heavy endpoints

**S3 optimization:**
- Lifecycle rules: IA after 30 days, Glacier after 90 days
- Use S3 Select to query subsets instead of downloading full objects
- Enable Intelligent-Tiering for unknown access patterns

**General:**
- Set up AWS Budgets alerts at 50%, 80%, 100% of expected spend
- Use Cost Explorer to identify top-spending services weekly
- Tag all resources for cost allocation
- Review unused resources monthly (orphaned EIPs, idle NAT Gateways)

A typical serverless startup spends 60% on compute (Lambda), 20% on storage (S3/DynamoDB), 10% on networking (NAT, data transfer), 10% on other services.

title: Terraform destroy fails with dependency errors and how to resolve
body:
Terraform destroy can fail when resources have implicit dependencies that Terraform doesn't track, like non-empty S3 buckets or Lambda@Edge functions.

**Error — non-empty S3 bucket:**
```
Error: deleting S3 Bucket (my-bucket): BucketNotEmpty: The bucket you tried to delete
is not empty. You must delete all versions in the bucket.
```

**Fix:**
```hcl
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "site" {
  bucket        = "my-bucket"
  force_destroy = true  # Deletes all objects when bucket is destroyed
}
```

**Error — Lambda@Edge replica:**
```
Error: deleting Lambda Function (us-east-1:my-edge-fn): InvalidParameterValueException:
 Lambda was unable to delete because it is a replicated function.
```

**Fix:** Wait 1-2 hours after removing the CloudFront association, then retry destroy. Lambda@Edge replicas take time to clean up.

**Error — circular dependency:**
```
Error: Cycle: aws_security_group.a, aws_security_group.b
```

**Fix — use separate rule resources:**
```hcl
resource "aws_security_group" "a" {
  name = "sg-a"
  vpc_id = var.vpc_id
  # Don't put ingress/egress rules inline
}

resource "aws_security_group_rule" "a_from_b" {
  type                     = "ingress"
  from_port                = 443
  to_port                  = 443
  protocol                 = "tcp"
  security_group_id        = aws_security_group.a.id
  source_security_group_id = aws_security_group.b.id
}
```

**Nuclear option for stuck resources:**
```bash
# Remove from state (resource still exists in AWS)
terraform state rm aws_s3_bucket.site
# Then delete manually via AWS console/CLI
```

Use `terraform destroy -target=aws_lambda_function.api` to destroy specific resources. Add `depends_on` for implicit dependencies that Terraform can't detect.
